turns.text: Hello Billie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s weird to be talking about this now, sitting here in this hospital bed with the monitor ticking away in the background. It feels like I’ve been driving forever, both on the road and in my head. Everyone keeps looking at me—at the "never married" box I had to check and the way I’m sitting here by myself—and they see a kid who’s in over her head. They see the nineteen-year-old, not the student nurse.

I’ve spent so many hours in clinical rotations watching these processes from the other side, but being the one in the gown is… it’s a different kind of terrifying. I know the protocol. I know the APGAR scale like the back of my hand, and I know exactly how critical that first Hepatitis B dose is for a newborn. I’ve read the studies, I’ve seen the risks, and I just feel this desperate need to make sure the staff realizes I’m not just being an anxious teen. I need them to listen to me like a colleague, not a patient they need to coddle or judge. I’m scared, yeah, but mostly I’m scared that they won’t treat his health with the same urgency I am. I just want him to be safe before I have to figure out how to do this whole "mom" thing alone.
Tell me about how you prepared for the birth — what did you hope for, and how did you go about planning for it?

Preparation felt like a second job, honestly. I was working full-time, trying to keep my head above water, and then spending every spare second scouring medical journals and databases. Most girls my age were looking at Pinterest for nursery themes, but I was obsessing over neonatal protocols. I wanted to be ready for the "what ifs," even if my own prenatal labs were clean. I kept thinking, *what if they miss something?* The thought of vertical transmission—that small window of time where I could protect him with the prophylaxis—it kept me up at night.

I had this "ideal" birth plan in my head, but I know how these things go. In clinicals, you learn that birth has a way of laughing at plans. I wanted a low-intervention birth, but more than that, I wanted someone to just *hear* me. I didn't want to be talked down to because of my age. I wanted to walk in there, hand over my knowledge, and have a partnership with the midwife. But now that I’m actually here, the reality is that I’m just isolated in this quiet rural town, trying to find a voice that isn’t shaking. I planned for the medicine, but I don't think I really planned for how lonely it feels when you're the one in the bed.
Tell me about the birth itself — take me through what happened, as much or as little as you'd like to share.

It was… blurry. Once the contractions really hit, the theory I’d spent months studying didn’t exactly make the pain vanish, but it gave me somewhere else to put my focus. I kept counting, timing them, trying to keep a clinical detachment even when my body felt like it was tearing apart. 

The midwife was nice enough, I guess, but that’s the thing—she was *nice* in a way that felt like she was talking to a child. When I finally brought up the Hepatitis B vaccine, her face shifted. She gave me that look again, the "don't worry, honey, we know what we're doing" look. It stung more than the contractions. I wanted to scream, *“I’ve done the rotations, I know the scheduling protocols better than you might think!”* but my voice just cracked.

When he finally came out, the room went from this high-stress, clinical chaos to this sudden, suffocating quiet. The relief of hearing him cry—that first, beautiful, healthy sound—it washed everything else away for a second. But even then, I wasn't just a mom looking at her baby; I was a nurse watching the clock. I held my breath until I saw the needle go in. When they finally let me do skin-to-skin, the cold of the room hit us, and I just focused on his temperature, his breathing. I’m exhausted, and I’m so scared of the drive back home, but he’s here. He’s safe. That’s all that matters, right?
At what moments during the birth did you feel most supported — and were there moments when you felt alone or unheard?

I felt supported in the most basic, biological way when they finally let me have him on my chest. That immediate skin-to-skin contact, feeling his heart rate settle against mine… for those few minutes, the medical hierarchy faded, and I was just his mom. It was the only time the room felt like it was actually *ours* instead of theirs.

But the rest of the time? I felt incredibly alone. It’s that invisible wall you hit when you’re nineteen and alone in a hospital. Every time I asked a question or pushed for a specific timing on a procedure, I could see the nurses exchange a glance. It wasn’t mean, necessarily, but it was dismissive. They’d nod and smile, the way you do when you’re humoring a child who’s read a little too much online. 

It was soul-crushing. I wasn't asking for special treatment; I was asking for the standard of care I’d been trained to uphold. Being treated like a "scared teen" meant my professional instincts were being ignored as "anxiety." It’s a bitter pill to swallow when you know you’re right, but you lack the status to be taken seriously. I walked in there hoping for a partner in his care, but I left feeling like I had to fight to be the advocate he needed. It made the whole experience feel like a hurdle I had to jump over, rather than a journey I was being supported through.
Tell me about pain management during your labour — what options you were offered, what you chose, and how that went.

I think because I was so focused on the clinical side of things—the "what ifs"—I was a bit stubborn about pain management. I wanted to stay as clear-headed as possible. Maybe that was my nurse brain kicking in, wanting to track my own progress and stay alert for any potential complications. I kept telling myself that I could handle the sensations if I just breathed through them the way I’d watched other women do during my rotations.

But the reality of labor is so much more intense than a textbook. When the pain really started to peak, the midwife offered me an epidural, and for a second, I felt like I was failing a test. I felt like if I took it, I was giving up control, or worse, proving that I couldn't "hack it" as a young, single mom. I think she saw that conflict on my face and, to her credit, she explained the benefits of being able to rest. 

In the end, I turned it down. I don't know if that was the "right" choice or just my pride talking, but I managed. It was agonizing, and there were moments I wanted to beg for relief, but I felt like if I stayed present, I wouldn't miss a single beat of his arrival. I just wish the *reason* I chose that felt more respected, instead of just being treated like I was being dramatic. It’s hard to feel like you’re making informed decisions when the people around you are already waiting for you to crumble.
Were there options you wanted that weren't available, or options you were steered towards that you weren't sure about?

I think the biggest gap wasn't a piece of equipment or a specific drug; it was the option to be treated as a peer. I kept looking for a conversation, you know? I wanted someone to sit down, look at my chart, and actually discuss the clinical rationale for the care path, not just give me the "I'm the professional, you're the patient" script.

I felt pushed toward a passive role the entire time. They wanted me to just follow instructions—*lay here, turn this way, stop asking questions*—because it made the room run smoother. Whenever I tried to bring up something more nuanced, like the timing of the vitamin K shot or the cord clamping, I could feel the tension in the room. They didn't explicitly say "no," but they steered me away by suggesting I was "overthinking" or just "very stressed." That dismissiveness is a powerful steering tool. It makes you feel like if you keep pushing, you're being "difficult," and in a hospital, being labeled "difficult" is the last thing you want when you’re already vulnerable.

I didn’t need extra bells and whistles. I just needed to be seen as a person who knows the stakes. It’s hard to advocate for your son when you’re being treated like a child yourself.
Tell me about the people who were with you during the birth — your partner, family, midwives, doctors — and what their presence meant to you.

There wasn't anyone in that room who truly knew *me*. No partner to hold my hand, no mom to squeeze my shoulder. My support system feels like a map with a lot of empty space. I have friends, but they’re all at school or working, and my family situation is… complicated. They’re proud of me for being a nurse-in-training, but they don't really get what this pregnancy means for my life, or how heavy the silence in my apartment gets at night. So, I walked into that delivery room as a party of one.

The nurses and the midwife were my entire world in that moment, for better or worse. Their presence was a paradox—they were the people I needed to keep my son alive, but they were also the people who made me feel most isolated. I watched them move around with this casual, routine confidence, and it just highlighted how small and alone I was. 

I’m not saying they were cruel—they did their jobs—but they were strangers. When I look back at those hours, I don’t see a team. I see a series of clinical interactions where I was a task to be completed. I didn't have anyone to advocate for me, so I had to be my own shield. It’s exhausting, trying to birth a baby while also trying to defend your own competence.
Was there a particular person — whether a professional or someone close to you — whose presence made a real difference? What did they do?

There was one nurse, toward the very end, who didn't seem to mind that I was a nineteen-year-old on my own. She was older, maybe in her fifties, and she had this quiet way about her. I was exhausted, shivering, and honestly just terrified that I was going to mess up the first time I held him. 

While she was prepping the vaccine—which, thank God, she actually listened to me about—she just leaned in and asked, "How did you learn all this?" Not in a way that was testing me, but like she was actually curious. When I told her about my clinicals and wanting to be a pediatric nurse, her whole demeanor changed. She didn't treat me like a kid anymore; she talked to me like a colleague. She started explaining what she was doing as she did it, not because I was a patient, but because she recognized I understood the language.

She was the only one who didn't roll her eyes or sigh when I asked a follow-up. For those ten or fifteen minutes, the isolation didn't feel so sharp. She didn't fix my life—I’m still terrified about the rent and the nursery—but she validated that I was capable. It was the only time I felt like I wasn't just a "case" on a chart.
Tell me about how the birth ended — whether that was the birth you expected, and how you felt in the immediate aftermath.

The birth ended with a weird mix of exhaustion and this sharp, frantic clarity. It wasn't the "peaceful" moment you see in movies, where everyone is smiling and the lighting is perfect. It was sterile, bright, and loud. 

I didn’t get the "natural" experience I’d romanticized in my head—sometimes nature just isn't enough, and my body was reaching its limit. When it was all over and he was finally here, I felt this overwhelming rush of adrenaline, but not the happy kind. It was the adrenaline of *responsibility*. I kept checking his color, watching his chest rise and fall, doing those mental assessments I learned in my classes. I think the doctors and nurses were ready for me to just "be the mom" and relax, but I couldn't. I was looking at him and seeing all the things that could go wrong in the next few hours, the next few days. 

In the immediate aftermath, I felt incredibly small in that big room. My son was there, and he was perfect, but the reality of my life—the rural roads, the single income, the cold apartment waiting for us—settled in right alongside the postpartum exhaustion. I did it. I got through the birth. But I felt like I’d just survived a crash course in adulthood, and I was still waiting for someone to tell me I’d actually passed.
If things didn't go to plan — or if there were interventions or complications — how were those explained or communicated to you at the time?

Communication was… fragmented. That’s the best way to put it. When things moved away from what I’d hoped for, the information was usually delivered in these short, clipped sentences. It was always, "We’re going to do this now," rather than, "Here is the data, here is why we’re suggesting this shift, and here is how you feel about it."

I remember the moment they suggested Pitocin to speed things along. It wasn't framed as a discussion; it was framed as a necessity because I was "stalling." They didn't ask me how my contractions felt or if I was struggling with the intensity; they just presented the outcome as a fact. Because I know the clinical side, I knew *why* they were suggesting it, but being treated like a bystander in my own labor was demoralizing. 

It was like they assumed I wouldn’t understand the science, so they just gave me the "patient version." Every time I tried to ask a clarifying question about the risks or the rationale, it was met with a patronizing, "Don't worry about the details, Billie, just focus on breathing." That hurt more than the actual labor. It turned a medical event into something where I was being managed, not cared for. It made me feel like my knowledge was an inconvenience to their process.
Tell me about the birth environment itself — the place where you gave birth, what it looked and felt like, and whether it felt like the right place for you.

The hospital was that sterile, beige kind of place where everything is designed to be easily wiped down. It was cold, too—the kind of cold that feels like it’s seeping through your bones when you’re barely wearing anything. There were monitors everywhere, beeping at intervals that made my heart race every time the sound changed. It wasn’t a sanctuary; it was a production line. 

Honestly, it felt like being in a lab, which I guess is what it *is*, but when you're the one in the center of it, you want it to feel a bit more human. The lighting was harsh and clinical, never really dimming enough to let me relax, and the walls had those generic, uplifting prints that felt so out of place with how frantic I felt inside.

Was it the right place? I don't know. My town is so isolated that I didn't exactly have a menu of options. I’m just thankful the facility was equipped to handle a delivery at all. But it felt alienating. I kept thinking about the difference between the hospital and a home, or even just a room that didn't feel like it was watching me. I wanted to be comfortable, but mostly, I just wanted to feel like I belonged there, and the entire environment seemed designed to make me feel like I was just passing through—a temporary occupant who didn't really have a say in the space.
How much choice did you have about where you gave birth — and did you have access to the options you wanted?

Choice? That’s a funny word when you look at a map of this area. When you're nineteen, living in a quiet rural town, and working a job that barely covers rent, your "options" are basically limited to whatever is reachable on a tank of gas. I didn't have a choice, really. There was the local hospital, and then there were hospitals an hour or two away that I definitely couldn't get to, especially not while driving myself through early labor. 

I dreamed about a birthing center—somewhere with softer lighting, someone who’d sit and actually *talk* to me instead of just rushing through a checklist—but those aren't exactly common around here. I was lucky just to have a hospital that offered standard maternity services. It felt like I was being funneled into a system that was built for efficiency, not for personal preference. 

I’m grateful for the medical safety net, don't get me wrong. I know how lucky I am that he’s healthy. But it wasn't the kind of choice a girl like me really gets to make. It was more about survival and proximity. You take what you can get, and you hope that the people in that building are at least competent enough to handle the things you’re too scared to voice.
Tell me about the first days and weeks after the birth — what that time was like for you, physically and emotionally.

The first few weeks were… they were a blur of adrenaline, exhaustion, and this heavy, constant weight in my chest. I remember bringing him home to my apartment, and the silence was so loud it was terrifying. It wasn't the peaceful quiet people talk about; it was the silence of *doing it alone*. Every time he’d make a sound in his sleep, my heart would jump into my throat, and I’d be checking him again—the color, the breathing, everything I’d studied in my rotations.

Physically, my body felt like it didn't belong to me anymore. I was recovering, sure, but I was also working through the adrenaline crash of the birth itself. I had to go back to thinking about shifts and income, even though I just wanted to stare at him. Emotionally, it was a rollercoaster. One minute I was so proud—I kept looking at him and thinking, *I grew this, I got us through the hospital, I’m his protector*—and the next minute, the reality of my age and the isolation would hit me like a physical blow. 

I’d catch myself sitting on the floor, waiting for the phone to ring or for someone to just stop by, but the days were mostly just us. It was lonely, and the fear of "what if" never really went away. It just changed shape.
What were the hardest parts of those early weeks — and were there things that helped you through them?

The hardest part was the quiet. When you’re in a hospital, even if you’re being ignored, there’s a hum of life—machines, voices, footsteps. Back in my apartment, it was just the ticking of the clock and his breathing. Every time I hit a snag—the breastfeeding struggles, the middle-of-the-night panic when he’d seem just a little too quiet—I had to stop myself from picking up the phone to call a supervisor or a mentor from my nursing rotations. I had to remember that I was the one in charge now. That realization was heavier than anything else.

The loneliness made everything feel like a mountain. When I was exhausted, I’d get these flashes of anger—at his father, at my situation, at the system that makes you grow up so fast—and then immediately feel guilty for not being the "perfect, serene" new mom. 

What helped? Oddly enough, it was the clinical routine I’d built. Sticking to a schedule for his feedings, tracking his weight, keeping a log—it gave me a sense of order when everything else felt chaotic. It made me feel like a nurse again, even when I was just a girl in pajamas. And sometimes, just getting out of the apartment, even if it was just for a walk in the rural air, helped clear the static in my head. It didn’t fix the isolation, but it reminded me that the world was still moving outside my door.
Tell me about feeding your baby — how you made decisions about it, and what the experience was actually like.

I think I had a very rigid, "textbook" view of it before he arrived. I knew the immunology behind breastfeeding—all those antibodies, the lower risk of infections—so I was convinced I was going to do it. It felt like another box to check, another way to ensure he got the best possible start. 

But when we were actually there, in the first few days, it wasn't this magical, effortless bond people talk about. It was physically exhausting and, honestly, a bit of a sensory nightmare. My nipples were cracked, my back was screaming, and he was so tiny and frantic, and I was so worried about whether he was actually getting enough. Every time he cried, my brain would immediately go to the potential for dehydration or latch issues—I was diagnosing him instead of just *holding* him.

It was lonely, too. Those late-night feeds at 3:00 AM are the absolute loneliest times in the world when it’s just you in a dark apartment. I remember staring at him and feeling like I was failing, just because I was so tired I couldn't think straight. I wanted someone to sit with me, maybe show me a better hold, or just tell me I was doing okay. Instead, I just had my notes and my own anxiety. It’s hard to make that connection feel natural when you’re treating it like a medical procedure you’re afraid to mess up.
Tell me about how you were feeling emotionally in the weeks and months after the birth — the full picture, not just the positive side.

It’s heavy. That’s the only way I can describe it. I think people expect you to be glowing or whatever, but I felt like I was holding my breath for months. I spent so much time being the "young, smart, capable" one in my nursing classes that when I finally became a mother, I felt like a fraud. I was terrified of every little cough, every sneeze, every shift in his breathing—I’d go down these mental rabbit holes, diagnosing him with everything from respiratory distress to something obscure I’d read about in a journal three years ago.

The isolation didn't just stay in the apartment; it followed me everywhere. I felt like I was living a double life—a teenager with a diaper bag and a pile of bills, and a pseudo-nurse trying to manage a household alone. I was angry, too. I’d be in the grocery store, looking at other parents who had partners or families with them, and I’d feel this sharp, jagged jealousy. Why did I have to do this without a safety net? Why did the system make me feel like I had to be a professional just to be taken seriously as a mom?

There were days when I’d just cry until my head hurt, not because I didn't love him, but because the weight of keeping him alive—completely, utterly alone—was just too much for a nineteen-year-old. I’m proud of what I did, and I love him more than anything, but the cost of that transition was a piece of my childhood I’ll never get back.
Tell me about your physical recovery after the birth — what that process was like and what support you had.

The recovery was... it was just another chore on an endless list, honestly. My body felt like it had been through a war, but I didn't have the luxury of taking the "me time" everyone talks about in the brochures. How do you recover when you’re the only person who can get up to change the diaper at 2:00 AM? How do you heal when you’re still trying to figure out how to pay the electric bill on a single, entry-level income?

I remember the physical pain of the healing process, the soreness that made every movement a conscious decision. I knew the signs of infection—I was checking myself for them every day, probably with more clinical detail than any doctor would expect—but I didn't have anyone to lean on. No one to bring me meals or take the baby for a few hours so I could actually sleep. 

My support was... basically nonexistent. It was just me, the internet, and a few medical textbooks I had left over from my classes. I think people looked at me and assumed that because I’m "young," I’d just bounce back, or that I had some secret reservoir of energy. I didn't. I was just running on adrenaline and the terrifying knowledge that if I collapsed, there was literally no one else there to pick him up. That thought is what kept me moving, even when I could barely stand.
Tell me about how your sense of yourself changed after you became a parent — your identity, your relationships, your daily life.

Before all this, I was defined by what I was going to be: a pediatric nurse. I was the one with the clinical rotations, the one who was going to "make it" out of this quiet, isolated town. When I became a mom, that identity—my future—suddenly felt like it was crashing into my present. Now, I’m "the nineteen-year-old mom" in the eyes of everyone who looks at me. I lost that ambition for a little while, not because I stopped caring, but because the daily survival of keeping a tiny human alive took up every ounce of my brainpower.

My relationships changed instantly. I’m nineteen, but I feel like I’m forty. Most of my friends are out doing the typical things—parties, school, just being young—and I’m here calculating nutrient intake and worrying about vaccines. I feel like I’m living on a different planet than them. It makes me feel bitter sometimes, and then incredibly guilty for that bitterness.

Every single day is a trade-off. Do I study for a certification so I can maybe get a better job, or do I take a nap because I’m so exhausted I can’t see straight? I’ve become this person who is constantly calculating the risks of everything. I’m not just Billie anymore; I’m a protective, scared, tired version of myself that I didn't even know existed.
Were there parts of your previous self — your work identity, social life, sense of independence — that felt hard to hold onto?

I think the hardest thing to let go of was the *future* version of myself. I was so used to being the girl with a plan—the one who was going to walk into the hospital as a professional, not a patient. When I became a mom, that sense of being a future colleague, an equal, just vanished. Now, when I walk into a clinic or a doctor’s office, I’m "that girl." Even if I use the right terminology, even if I know exactly why they’re checking his reflexes, they look at me like I’m reciting lines I don't understand. It’s infuriating. 

My social life? It basically evaporated. How do I relate to friends who are worried about a pop quiz when I’m worried about whether I can afford the next round of vaccines or if the baby’s fever is something I need to take him into the ER for? I feel like I’m playing a game where the rules changed, but everyone else is still on the original board.

The independence part is the biggest irony. I’m doing everything on my own—managing the bills, the health, the baby, the rent—yet I’ve never felt less independent. I’m tethered to this apartment, this schedule, and this constant, nagging fear. I’m independent in the "doing" sense, but I’m desperately craving the kind of freedom where I can just be a person, not a parent-in-a-crisis. It’s like I’ve become this efficient little machine, and I really miss being just… Billie.
Tell me about returning to work — or thinking about returning to work — and what that transition was like for you.

Returning to work is this constant, gnawing anxiety in the back of my mind. I’m staring at my textbooks, the ones I haven't cracked in months because I’m so drained, and I feel this push-pull dynamic that’s tearing me apart. On one hand, I’m desperate to finish my clinicals and get my license—because that’s my lifeline, that’s my way out of this quiet, rural trap and into a life where I’m not just a teenager in a gown. I want to be in scrubs, doing the work, being the one people listen to instead of the one they dismiss.

But on the other hand, the thought of leaving him? It makes my chest tighten. Even if I could find childcare that’s actually affordable—which feels like a myth in this town—who’s going to look at him the way I do? Who’s going to check his skin for jaundice or his cry for signs of distress the way his mom would? 

I know I have to go back. I have to work to pay for the roof over our heads and the vaccines that keep him safe. But the transition feels like I’m abandoning my role as his protector to go back to being a student who’s still fighting to be taken seriously. It’s not just about a job; it’s about proving to everyone—and mostly to myself—that I can actually do both. And that scares me more than anything.
How did you feel about going back — was it something you looked forward to, something you dreaded, or both?

It’s a tangled mess of both, honestly. I look at my textbooks and I feel this hunger—I want to be in the clinic, I want the structure, and I desperately need to prove that I’m more than just a girl who got pregnant too soon. I want to be the one giving the care, the one whose opinion matters when it comes to a kid’s health. Being in that professional space is the only way I can see myself building a life that feels solid and secure, and that’s a powerful motivator.

But then I look at him, and the dread just hits like a wave. I think about the sterile hospital rooms and the way the staff treated me, and I’m terrified that if I go back to that world—even as a student nurse—I’ll still be seen through that same narrow lens. Will they let me be a nurse, or will they only ever see the nineteen-year-old who "made a mistake"? 

And the logistics of it… the guilt of leaving him, the fear that something will happen while I’m not there to watch him, the constant ticking clock of the bills—it’s all so much. I’m torn between wanting to reclaim my life and feeling like I’m abandoning my baby to a world that doesn’t know how to care for us properly. It’s a transition I *have* to make to survive, but it feels like walking into a storm.
Tell me about how you used digital tools, apps, forums, or online communities after the birth — what role they played in your life as a new parent.

The internet was my lifeline, for better or worse. In the middle of the night, when I was sitting in the dark and the silence felt like it was pressing in on me, my phone was the only thing connecting me to a world where I didn't feel so alone. I lived on forums, searching through old threads about infant development and vaccination schedules. 

There was a real comfort in the anonymity of it. I could post a question about a feeding issue or a strange rash and, for a few minutes, I wasn't "the nineteen-year-old single mom" to the people replying. I was just someone looking for information. It gave me a way to validate my own instincts without having to deal with the pitying looks I got at the pediatrician’s office.

But it was also a double-edged sword. Sometimes I’d go down these rabbit holes, reading horror stories about rare complications, and my anxiety would spike until I was trembling. I had to learn how to filter the noise. It wasn't a replacement for real, human support—I’d give anything for a mentor to sit with me in my apartment—but in a rural town where I feel like I’m the only one struggling to balance a nursing career and a baby, the digital world was the only place I could find people who understood the stakes.
Were there online communities or social media groups that became a significant source of support or information? What did they offer you?

I spent a lot of time on specialized nursing student forums and some of the more evidence-based parenting groups. Those were my sanity checks. In the parenting groups, I could search for specific clinical concerns—like whether a slightly elevated temp was cause for concern or just post-vaccination side effects—and get answers that weren't wrapped in "honey, don't worry about it." It was like having a peer-to-peer consultation without the condescension.

The nursing student forums were different; they were about holding onto my ambition. Reading about people who balanced clinicals with life, even if they weren't in my exact situation, made the goal of finishing my degree feel like it was still physically possible. It was a way to keep my brain in "student mode" even when I was stuck at home in pajamas. 

But honestly? Even with those groups, there was still that sting of distance. Nobody there was sitting in my living room. I could type out my fears about making rent or the crushing weight of being nineteen and on my own, and someone would send a "virtual hug" emoji, but that doesn't actually hold a crying baby or change a diaper. It was a lifeline for my brain, but it couldn't touch the isolation of the day-to-day. It’s information, not community. There’s a massive difference.
Tell me about how you would describe your overall experience of maternity care — from preconception through to now. What would you want future services to know?

If I had to describe it, I’d say it felt like I was navigating a system that didn't know what to do with a person who existed in two worlds at once: the patient world and the professional one. The care I received was clinical, yes, but it was hollow. It lacked the one thing that truly matters when you're terrified and alone: dignity.

I want future services to know that when a nineteen-year-old walks into your care, you don't need to pity her. You don't need to "manage" her. You need to *hear* her. Just because I’m young, or because my marital status is what it is, doesn't mean I don't understand the risks or the protocols. My "anxiety" wasn't just being a dramatic kid; it was the baseline stress of being a mother who knows enough to know what can go wrong, and having no one else to share that burden with.

Stop talking down to us. When you see a young woman, look for the person underneath the chart. If you had treated me like a colleague—a peer—instead of a checklist item, I might have felt like a partner in my son’s birth instead of an inconvenience to yours. We don't need to be "humored." We need to be respected, listened to, and—above all—seen for exactly who we are.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the only thing I’d add is that people act like the "clinical" part is the only part that matters in a hospital. They think if the vitals are stable and the APGAR is good, they’ve done their job perfectly. But they don't seem to realize that the memory of how I was treated—the dismissiveness, the pitying looks—is going to stay with me for as long as I’m a nurse. 

When I finally get my degree and walk onto a unit, I’m going to remember exactly how small the staff made me feel. I’m going to see that "never married" or "nineteen" on a chart and think, *that girl knows exactly what’s happening, and she’s probably scared to death, so listen to her.* 

I think the biggest thing that gets lost in all these protocols is that when you provide care to someone, you’re not just treating a body; you’re leaving a mark on their life. I really hope that for the next girl who walks into that room—who’s as terrified and alone as I was—the staff decides to listen before they decide to judge. I just hope they can be better to her than they were to me. Thank you for asking. It’s been… heavy to say all this out loud, but I’m glad someone finally wanted to hear it.
